Summary: The paper introduces CostRouter, a per-query routing system for multi-agent LLM deployments that selects the cheapest topology (flat, hierarchical, or debate) meeting a quality threshold. The main contributions are: (1) a topology cost algebra predicting token expenditure from structural parameters with R² > 0.95, (2) CostRouter using a lightweight MLP classifier for difficulty estimation with pre-computed accuracy profiles, and (3) empirical validation across 82 tasks showing 42% token cost reduction at τ=0.6 while losing only 1.5 percentage points of accuracy versus always-debate. The system operates orthogonally to model-level routing (FrugalGPT), addressing structure rather than model selection.
Strengths: Novel problem framing: topology cost as structural and predictable, orthogonal to model selection
Clean mathematical formulation of topology cost algebra with strong empirical fit (R² > 0.95)
Practical system design with minimal overhead (<0.1% of total task cost, <2ms routing latency)
Good empirical validation across diverse task categories (coding, reasoning, research)
Clear theoretical bound on cost savings relative to oracle routing
Well-acknowledged limitations and thoughtful future directions
Weaknesses: Single backbone evaluation (Claude Opus 4.6) - generalization to other LLMs unvalidated
Small test set (22 tasks) with single-run evaluation - insufficient statistical power for precise claims
Difficulty estimator achieves only 84% accuracy on binary easy/hard split, trained on 282 examples
Large accuracy gap between Oracle (90%) and CostRouter (63.1%) suggests significant routing error
Cold-start problem for τ calibration not adequately addressed
Cost algebra assumes fixed token prices, but real APIs have dynamic pricing
No mid-task topology adaptation - routing is one-shot per query
Questions: How does CostRouter's performance generalize across different LLM backbones (GPT-4o, Claude Sonnet, open-source models)? The paper acknowledges this limitation but it's critical for practical deployment.

What explains the large gap between Oracle (90% accuracy) and CostRouter (63.1% accuracy)? Is this primarily due to difficulty estimation errors, accuracy profile miscalibration, or fundamental limitations of the routing approach?

The difficulty estimator uses 282 training examples with 84% accuracy on binary classification. How robust is this to distribution shift? What happens with query types not represented in OrchestraBench?
For cold-start deployments with no historical accuracy data, how should practitioners set τ? The paper recommends defaulting to debate, but this largely defeats the purpose.

The paper claims the cost algebra is backbone-independent. Can you provide experiments showing that token count predictions hold across different model families with different context handling behaviors?
Limitations: Single backbone evaluation limits generalizability claims
Small test set (22 tasks) with single-run evaluation lacks statistical rigor
Difficulty estimator trained on limited data may not generalize to novel task types
Cost algebra assumes fixed token pricing, inconsistent with real-world API dynamics
No mid-task topology switching - routing commits to single topology upfront
Oracle achieves 90% accuracy vs CostRouter's 63.1%, indicating significant room for improvement
Cold-start τ calibration requires conservative defaults, delaying cost benefits
